title: Scaphoid Fracture
oneLiner: The most commonly fractured carpal bone, typically from a FOOSH injury; missed fractures risk avascular necrosis and nonunion, leading to chronic wrist pain and arthritis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 22-year-old male presents to the ED after slipping on ice and landing on his outstretched right hand. He complains of dorsal wrist pain and swelling, especially on the radial side. He has difficulty gripping, and there is tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Initial wrist X-rays appear normal, but because of high clinical suspicion, he is immobilised in a thumb spica cast and referred for an MRI, which confirms a scaphoid waist fracture.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **FOOSH** (fall onto outstretched hand) — extends and radially deviates the wrist, forcing the scaphoid against the radius.
- **Dorsal radial wrist pain** — typical location; the scaphoid spans the proximal and distal carpal rows, so pain is maximal over the snuffbox.
- **Tenderness in anatomical snuffbox** — the scaphoid lies directly beneath; this sign has >90% sensitivity for acute fracture.
- **Normal initial X-rays** — up to 20% of scaphoid fractures are radiographically occult at presentation; rely on clinical exam and advanced imaging.
- **Young male** — highest incidence in males aged 15–40, especially in sports or high-energy falls.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **The "wrist sprain" that never gets better** — patient presents weeks later with persistent radial wrist pain, often dismissed as a simple sprain. Look for old X-ray findings or MRI signs of nonunion.
- **The fall with minimal symptoms** — elderly patients may have low-energy falls and minimal swelling; scaphoid fracture can mimic arthritis.
- **Pain primarily over the scaphoid tubercle (volar prominence)** — less common but can occur with distal pole fractures; may be mistaken for thenar muscle strain.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
A scaphoid fracture is a break in the scaphoid bone, the most commonly fractured carpal bone (accounting for ~70% of carpal fractures). It typically results from a fall onto an outstretched hand, with the wrist in dorsiflexion and radial deviation.  
**Why it matters:** Because the scaphoid has a tenuous retrograde blood supply from the radial artery (entering distally), fractures through the proximal pole or waist can interrupt perfusion, leading to **avascular necrosis (AVN)**. Missed or inadequately treated fractures go on to nonunion or malunion, causing chronic pain, reduced grip strength, and post-traumatic osteoarthritis. Up to 12% of scaphoid fractures are initially missed on plain X-rays, making clinical vigilance essential. A junior doctor's ability to recognise the pattern, initiate immobilisation, and arrange appropriate follow-up can prevent lifelong disability.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of the fracture:** When you fall onto an outstretched hand, the wrist extends and radially deviates. The scaphoid, which spans both the proximal and distal carpal rows, is forced into extreme dorsiflexion. The waist of the scaphoid contacts the dorsal rim of the radius, acting as a fulcrum, and the bone snaps. The fracture line most often occurs at the waist (70–80%), but can also involve the proximal pole (10–20%) or distal pole (10–20%).

**Why you get specific signs:**
- **Anatomical snuffbox tenderness** — The scaphoid lies deep to the snuffbox, bounded by extensor pollicis longus (medially) and extensor pollicis brevis/abductor pollicis longus (laterally). Fracture causes localised tenderness that is reproducible and distinct from other structures.
- **Pain on axial compression of the thumb** — Joint distraction compresses the scaphoid between the radius and the trapezium; if transduced along the scaphoid, the fracture site experiences painful movement.
- **Swelling over the radial dorsum** — Haemorrhage and inflammation from the fracture effaces the normal snuffbox concavity.
- **Scaphoid tubercle tenderness and pain on pronation** — The distal pole articulates with the trapezium; motion at that joint reproduces symptoms.

**Why missing it leads to trouble:** The blood supply enters via the **dorsal ridge** (lateral scaphoid branch of radial artery) and **distal pole** (volar branch). Proximal pole receives flow only through the scaphoid itself. A waist fracture disrupts the intraosseous vessels, leaving the proximal fragment entirely dependent on the tenuous retrograde supply. This is why proximal fractures have the highest risk of AVN. Nonunion occurs when the fracture gap is >1 mm or there is persistent motion across the fracture; the fibrous tissue that forms is unable to undergo endochondral ossification without proper immobilisation or fixation.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
- **Traumatic (most common)**  
  - *Fall onto outstretched hand* (FOOSH) — "slip on ice, fall from a height, sports"  
  - *Direct blow* — e.g., punching a wall (rare but described)  
  - *High-energy motor vehicle collision* — often accompanied by other carpal or radial fractures (e.g., trans-scaphoid perilunate dislocation)
- **Stress (rare)**  
  - *Repetitive loading* — gymnasts, weightlifters; presents with insidious pain. Stem clue: "no acute fall, progressive wrist pain over weeks."
- **Pathological**  
  - *Pre-existing cyst or tumour* (very rare) — look for lucent lesion on X-ray.
- **Iatrogenic**  
  - *Postoperative* — e.g., after scaphoid bone grafting.

**Most common cause:** FOOSH. Always ask about fall direction, surface, hand dominance.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis is based on clinical findings plus imaging. A high clinical suspicion with negative X-rays warrants further imaging.

**Tests in order of usual sequence:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Plain X-rays (wrist 4 views)** | AP, lateral, oblique, scaphoid view (ulnar deviation) | Initial screening; identify fracture line, displacement, or bone gap | Clear fracture line = confirmed; but up to 20% false negative. Look for the "scaphoid fat pad stripe" (displacement of pronator quadratus) as a soft-tissue sign. |
| **MRI** | Bone marrow oedema, fracture line, AVN (marrow fat signal changes) | Gold standard for occult fracture; also shows ligamentous injury | High sensitivity (~100%) for fracture; if negative, no further workup needed. Can assess AVN: T1-weighted loss of marrow signal is concerning. |
| **CT** | Cortical disruption, displacement, angulation, nonunion | Preoperative planning, assessment of union; better than X-ray for displacement | Nonunion: cystic change, sclerotic margins, gap >1 mm. Displacement: step >1 mm or dorsal/radial angulation. |
| **Ultrasound** | Cortical step, fluid, hyperaemia | Emerging bedside tool; operator-dependent | Less accurate but can detect fracture in some hands; not standard. |

**Single finding that confirms:** A clearly visible fracture line on X-ray or MRI.  
**Single finding that rules out:** Negative MRI with no bone oedema or fracture line.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Analgesia** — paracetamol 1 g PO (or IV if NBM) + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS) unless contraindicated. Avoid opioids if possible.
- **Splintage** — apply a **thumb spica splint** (below-elbow cast incorporating the thumb to immobilise the first metacarpal). Rationale: immobilises the scaphoid by limiting wrist and thumb motion. If a formal cast isn't available, a dorsal wrist splint with thumb extension is a temporising measure.
- **Elevation** — sling or elevation above heart to minimise swelling.
- **Referral** — to fracture clinic or orthopaedic hand team within 24–48 hours.

**Ward / Outpatient (24–72 hours):**
- **Definitive casting** — if nondisplaced: **scaphoid cast** (long-arm thumb spica) or modern short-arm thumb spica. Traditionally, waist fractures are casted for 6–12 weeks, but recent evidence supports shorter immobilisation (6–8 weeks) with MRI follow-up. Always specify: below-elbow, thumb spica.
- **Repeat imaging** — after cast removal, X-ray to assess union. If uncertain, CT or MRI.
- **Surgical fixation** — indications: **displacement >1 mm**, **proximal pole fracture**, **delayed presentation (>4 weeks)**, **nonunion**. Usually done with a headless compression screw (Herbert or Acutrak).  
- **Antibiotics** — not routine unless open fracture (rare).
- **Pain management** — continue analgesia; consider neuropathic component (e.g., CRPS prophylaxis with vitamin C 500 mg PO daily for 50 days — reduces risk of complex regional pain syndrome, especially after surgery).

**Long-term (weeks to months):**
- **Rehabilitation** — hand therapy after cast removal: range-of-motion exercises, then strengthening. Return to sport typically 8–12 weeks.
- **Follow-up** — serial X-rays at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and until union confirmed. Nonunion patients require bone grafting + screw fixation.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not accept "wrist sprain" without careful examination and MRI if suspicious — a missed scaphoid fracture is a common medicolegal pitfall.
  - Do not routinely request CT as first-line — it exposes to radiation and may miss oedema. Use MRI for occult fractures.
  - Do not remove the cast before 6 weeks without confirming union.
  - Do not ignore pain that persists after cast removal — could be nonunion or AVN.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Scaphoid Fracture | Scapholunate Dissociation | Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex (TFCC) Tear | Wrist Sprain (General) |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain location** | Radial, snuffbox | Radial, dorsal, "clunk" on movement | Ulnar side, with pronation/supination | Variable, often diffuse |
| **Examination** | Snuffbox tenderness, axial compression pain | Scapholunate ballotment (Watson test): pain and "clunk"; scaphoid shift test positive | Ulnar fovea tenderness, "shuck" test, grind test; may have instability | Generalised tenderness, no specific provocative sign |
| **Key imaging** | Fracture line on X-ray or MRI; MRI shows oedema | X-ray: scapholunate gap >3 mm (Terry-Thomas sign); MRI: tear on arthrogram or MR arthrography | MRI: tears of the ulnar-sided complex, often with foveal detachment | Normal X-ray and MRI; diagnosis of exclusion |
| **Special features** | High risk of AVN and nonunion if missed | Causes dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI); chronic leads to scapholunate advanced collapse (SLAC) | "Snapping" or clicking with forearm rotation; can follow fall or twisting injury | Self-limiting, no long-term deficits |
| **Key differentiator** | Tenderness directly over scaphoid; positive axial compression of thumb | Scapholunate gap on clenched-fist AP view | Ulnar-sided pain with foveal tenderness; MRI confirms tear | Normal advanced imaging; rapid resolution |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Nonunion** (5–15%)  
  *Presentation:* Persistent pain and tenderness beyond 8–12 weeks despite immobilisation. X-ray shows a gap, cystic changes, or sclerotic margins.  
  *What to do:* CT for detailed assessment; refer for surgical fixation with bone grafting (typically from distal radius or iliac crest). Nonunion leads to progressive collapse.
- **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** (10–30% of proximal pole fractures)  
  *Presentation:* Delayed; often discovered on follow-up X-rays or MRI as increased bone density (sclerosis) and collapse. Patient may have had initial fracture treated conservatively.  
  *What to do:* MRI is best for early detection. If AVN is present, options include: vascularised bone grafts (e.g., 1,2 intercompartmental supraretinacular artery graft) or salvage procedures (proximal row carpectomy, scaphoid excision, four-corner fusion).
- **Post-traumatic osteoarthritis** (common if nonunion or AVN persists)  
  *Presentation:* Chronic wrist pain, decreased range of motion, radiographic joint space narrowing, osteophytes. Typically develops 5–10 years after injury.  
  *What to do:* NSAIDs, activity modification, wrist arthroscopy for debridement; eventual fusion for severe cases.
- **Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)**  
  *Presentation:* Burning pain, swelling, vasomotor changes (warmth/coolness), sudomotor changes (sweating), trophic changes. More common after surgery.  
  *What to do:* early recognition, physiotherapy, vitamin C 500 mg daily for 50 days post-injury (prophylaxis), gabapentin/pregabalin for established CRPS, consider stellate ganglion block.

**Late-presentation patterns:** A 45-year-old woman presents with "wrist pain for 6 months after a minor fall she dismissed." X-ray shows a nonunited scaphoid waist fracture with cystic changes and early AVN of the proximal pole. The stem tests "what would have been the correct initial management?" Answer: immobilisation and MRI.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **SNAC** — *Scaphoid Nonunion Advanced Collapse* pattern of wrist arthritis (stage 1: styloid involvement; stage 2: radioscaphoid; stage 3: capitate involvement; stage 4: pan-carpal).
- **A, B, C, D, E** of scaphoid fracture workup:  
  A — Anatomical snuffbox tenderness  
  B — Bone scan / MRI for occult  
  C — Casting (thumb spica)  
  D — Displacement >1 mm = surgery  
  E — Early referral to orthopaedics  

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Most sensitive physical exam sign:** Tenderness at the anatomical snuffbox > scaphoid tubercle tenderness > axial compression of thumb. But a combination of two or more signs improves accuracy.
2. **Never accept a "normal X-ray" as ruling out scaphoid fracture** if mechanism and exam fit. The "scaphoid fat pad stripe" on a lateral X-ray (a thin lucency volar to the scaphoid) is a soft-tissue sign of haemorrhage; if present, treat as fracture.
3. **MRI is the gold standard** for occult fractures — it shows bone oedema within hours. CT is better for evaluating nonunion or displacement but not for acute diagnosis.
4. **Proximal pole fractures are emergencies** — they have the highest AVN risk and usually need prompt surgical fixation even if nondisplaced.
5. **A delay in diagnosis >4 weeks significantly increases nonunion risk** — up to 88% nonunion vs. 10% if treated early.
6. **Thumb spica is not always enough** — waist fractures may require a long-arm thumb spica for the first 6 weeks to immobilise elbow supination/pronation (which strains the scaphoid). Recent evidence is shifting but still debated.
7. **Vitamin C prophylaxis for CRPS** — give 500 mg daily for 50 days to any patient with a wrist fracture (scaphoid or distal radius) to reduce CRPS incidence.

**Common mistakes:**
- Forgetting to order a specific scaphoid view (ulnar deviation AP) on X-ray.
- Assuming a scaphoid fracture is "just a wrist sprain" because they're young.
- Not arranging a follow-up MRI for a clinically suspicious but X-ray-negative wrist.
- Mobilising a patient with a cast and no follow-up plan — they need clear return-to-clinic instructions.
- Missing associated injuries (e.g., trans-scaphoid perilunate dislocation) — look for lunate dislocation on lateral X-ray.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (orthopaedic registrar) immediately for:**
  - Open fracture (rare but possible)
  - Neurovascular compromise (median/radial nerve symptoms, absent radial pulse)
  - Suspected compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive extension of fingers)
  - Displaced fracture (step >1 mm or angulation) on X-ray — needs urgent reduction and fixation
  - Proximal pole fracture — discuss within 24 hours for possible acute fixation
- **Refer to hand surgery clinic for:**
  - All confirmed scaphoid fractures (within 1–2 days for cast vs. surgery decision)
  - Persistent nonunion or AVN findings
- **Escalate to ICU/critical care if:** 
  - Septic shock from an open fracture (very rare)
  - Massive haemorrhage from associated radial artery injury (extremely rare)
- **Thresholds to act on:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** — escalate to ward registrar; if ≥ 7, consider critical care
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in trauma context — may indicate arterial injury or compartment syndrome
  - **GCS drop** — never a direct complication of scaphoid fracture, but if associated with head injury from the same fall, escalate accordingly

## Scaphoid Fracture
**One-liner:** The most commonly fractured carpal bone, typically from a FOOSH injury; missed fractures risk avascular necrosis and nonunion, leading to chronic wrist pain and arthritis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
A 22-year-old male presents to the ED after slipping on ice and landing on his outstretched right hand. He complains of dorsal wrist pain and swelling, especially on the radial side. He has difficulty gripping, and there is tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Initial wrist X-rays appear normal, but because of high clinical suspicion, he is immobilised in a thumb spica cast and referred for an MRI, which confirms a scaphoid waist fracture.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **FOOSH** (fall onto outstretched hand) — extends and radially deviates the wrist, forcing the scaphoid against the radius.
- **Dorsal radial wrist pain** — typical location; the scaphoid spans the proximal and distal carpal rows, so pain is maximal over the snuffbox.

- **Normal initial X-rays** — up to 20% of scaphoid fractures are radiographically occult at presentation; rely on clinical exam and advanced imaging.
- **Young male** — highest incidence in males aged 15–40, especially in sports or high-energy falls.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **The "wrist sprain" that never gets better** — patient presents weeks later with persistent radial wrist pain, often dismissed as a simple sprain. Look for old X-ray findings or MRI signs of nonunion.
- **The fall with minimal symptoms** — elderly patients may have low-energy falls and minimal swelling; scaphoid fracture can mimic arthritis.
- **Pain primarily over the scaphoid tubercle (volar prominence)** — less common but can occur with distal pole fractures; may be mistaken for thenar muscle strain.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
A scaphoid fracture is a break in the scaphoid bone, the most commonly fractured carpal bone (accounting for ~70% of carpal fractures). It typically results from a fall onto an outstretched hand, with the wrist in dorsiflexion and radial deviation.  
**Why it matters:** Because the scaphoid has a tenuous retrograde blood supply from the radial artery (entering distally), fractures through the proximal pole or waist can interrupt perfusion, leading to **avascular necrosis (AVN)**. Missed or inadequately treated fractures go on to nonunion or malunion, causing chronic pain, reduced grip strength, and post-traumatic osteoarthritis. Up to 12% of scaphoid fractures are initially missed on plain X-rays, making clinical vigilance essential. A junior doctor's ability to recognise the pattern, initiate immobilisation, and arrange appropriate follow-up can prevent lifelong disability.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story of the fracture:** When you fall onto an outstretched hand, the wrist extends and radially deviates. The scaphoid, which spans both the proximal and distal carpal rows, is forced into extreme dorsiflexion. The waist of the scaphoid contacts the dorsal rim of the radius, acting as a fulcrum, and the bone snaps. The fracture line most often occurs at the waist (70–80%), but can also involve the proximal pole (10–20%) or distal pole (10–20%).

**Why you get specific signs:**
- **Anatomical snuffbox tenderness** — The scaphoid lies deep to the snuffbox, bounded by extensor pollicis longus (medially) and extensor pollicis brevis/abductor pollicis longus (laterally). Fracture causes localised tenderness that is reproducible and distinct from other structures.
- **Pain on axial compression of the thumb** — Joint distraction compresses the scaphoid between the radius and the trapezium; if transduced along the scaphoid, the fracture site experiences painful movement.
- **Swelling over the radial dorsum** — Haemorrhage and inflammation from the fracture effaces the normal snuffbox concavity.
- **Scaphoid tubercle tenderness and pain on pronation** — The distal pole articulates with the trapezium; motion at that joint reproduces symptoms.

**Why missing it leads to trouble:** The blood supply enters via the **dorsal ridge** (lateral scaphoid branch of radial artery) and **distal pole** (volar branch). Proximal pole receives flow only through the scaphoid itself. A waist fracture disrupts the intraosseous vessels, leaving the proximal fragment entirely dependent on the tenuous retrograde supply. This is why proximal fractures have the highest risk of AVN. Nonunion occurs when the fracture gap is >1 mm or there is persistent motion across the fracture; the fibrous tissue that forms is unable to undergo endochondral ossification without proper immobilisation or fixation.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
- **Traumatic (most common)**  
  - *Fall onto outstretched hand* (FOOSH) — "slip on ice, fall from a height, sports"  
  - *Direct blow* — e.g., punching a wall (rare but described)  
  - *High-energy motor vehicle collision* — often accompanied by other carpal or radial fractures (e.g., trans-scaphoid perilunate dislocation)
- **Stress (rare)**  
  - *Repetitive loading* — gymnasts, weightlifters; presents with insidious pain. Stem clue: "no acute fall, progressive wrist pain over weeks."
- **Pathological**  
  - *Pre-existing cyst or tumour* (very rare) — look for lucent lesion on X-ray.
- **Iatrogenic**  
  - *Postoperative* — e.g., after scaphoid bone grafting.

**Most common cause:** FOOSH. Always ask about fall direction, surface, hand dominance.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis is based on clinical findings plus imaging. A high clinical suspicion with negative X-rays warrants further imaging.

**Tests in order of usual sequence:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Plain X-rays (wrist 4 views)** | AP, lateral, oblique, scaphoid view (ulnar deviation) | Initial screening; identify fracture line, displacement, or bone gap | Clear fracture line = confirmed; but up to 20% false negative. Look for the "scaphoid fat pad stripe" (displacement of pronator quadratus) as a soft-tissue sign. |
| **MRI** | Bone marrow oedema, fracture line, AVN (marrow fat signal changes) | Gold standard for occult fracture; also shows ligamentous injury | High sensitivity (~100%) for fracture; if negative, no further workup needed. Can assess AVN: T1-weighted loss of marrow signal is concerning. |
| **CT** | Cortical disruption, displacement, angulation, nonunion | Preoperative planning, assessment of union; better than X-ray for displacement | Nonunion: cystic change, sclerotic margins, gap >1 mm. Displacement: step >1 mm or dorsal/radial angulation. |
| **Ultrasound** | Cortical step, fluid, hyperaemia | Emerging bedside tool; operator-dependent | Less accurate but can detect fracture in some hands; not standard. |

**Single finding that confirms:** A clearly visible fracture line on X-ray or MRI.  
**Single finding that rules out:** Negative MRI with no bone oedema or fracture line.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Analgesia** — paracetamol 1 g PO (or IV if NBM) + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO TDS) unless contraindicated. Avoid opioids if possible.
- **Splintage** — apply a **thumb spica splint** (below-elbow cast incorporating the thumb to immobilise the first metacarpal). Rationale: immobilises the scaphoid by limiting wrist and thumb motion. If a formal cast isn't available, a dorsal wrist splint with thumb extension is a temporising measure.
- **Elevation** — sling or elevation above heart to minimise swelling.
- **Referral** — to fracture clinic or orthopaedic hand team within 24–48 hours.

**Ward / Outpatient (24–72 hours):**
- **Definitive casting** — if nondisplaced: **scaphoid cast** (long-arm thumb spica) or modern short-arm thumb spica. Traditionally, waist fractures are casted for 6–12 weeks, but recent evidence supports shorter immobilisation (6–8 weeks) with MRI follow-up. Always specify: below-elbow, thumb spica.
- **Repeat imaging** — after cast removal, X-ray to assess union. If uncertain, CT or MRI.
- **Surgical fixation** — indications: **displacement >1 mm**, **proximal pole fracture**, **delayed presentation (>4 weeks)**, **nonunion**. Usually done with a headless compression screw (Herbert or Acutrak).  
- **Antibiotics** — not routine unless open fracture (rare).
- **Pain management** — continue analgesia; consider neuropathic component (e.g., CRPS prophylaxis with vitamin C 500 mg PO daily for 50 days — reduces risk of complex regional pain syndrome, especially after surgery).

**Long-term (weeks to months):**
- **Rehabilitation** — hand therapy after cast removal: range-of-motion exercises, then strengthening. Return to sport typically 8–12 weeks.
- **Follow-up** — serial X-rays at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and until union confirmed. Nonunion patients require bone grafting + screw fixation.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not accept "wrist sprain" without careful examination and MRI if suspicious — a missed scaphoid fracture is a common medicolegal pitfall.
  - Do not routinely request CT as first-line — it exposes to radiation and may miss oedema. Use MRI for occult fractures.
  - Do not remove the cast before 6 weeks without confirming union.
  - Do not ignore pain that persists after cast removal — could be nonunion or AVN.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Scaphoid Fracture | Scapholunate Dissociation | Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex (TFCC) Tear | Wrist Sprain (General) |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain location** | Radial, snuffbox | Radial, dorsal, "clunk" on movement | Ulnar side, with pronation/supination | Variable, often diffuse |
| **Examination** | Snuffbox tenderness, axial compression pain | Scapholunate ballotment (Watson test): pain and "clunk"; scaphoid shift test positive | Ulnar fovea tenderness, "shuck" test, grind test; may have instability | Generalised tenderness, no specific provocative sign |
| **Key imaging** | Fracture line on X-ray or MRI; MRI shows oedema | X-ray: scapholunate gap >3 mm (Terry-Thomas sign); MRI: tear on arthrogram or MR arthrography | MRI: tears of the ulnar-sided complex, often with foveal detachment | Normal X-ray and MRI; diagnosis of exclusion |
| **Special features** | High risk of AVN and nonunion if missed | Causes dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI); chronic leads to scapholunate advanced collapse (SLAC) | "Snapping" or clicking with forearm rotation; can follow fall or twisting injury | Self-limiting, no long-term deficits |
| **Key differentiator** | Tenderness directly over scaphoid; positive axial compression of thumb | Scapholunate gap on clenched-fist AP view | Ulnar-sided pain with foveal tenderness; MRI confirms tear | Normal advanced imaging; rapid resolution | 

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Nonunion** (5–15%)  
  *Presentation:* Persistent pain and tenderness beyond 8–12 weeks despite immobilisation. X-ray shows a gap, cystic changes, or sclerotic margins.  
  *What to do:* CT for detailed assessment; refer for surgical fixation with bone grafting (typically from distal radius or iliac crest). Nonunion leads to progressive collapse.
- **Avascular necrosis (AVN)** (10–30% of proximal pole fractures)  
  *Presentation:* Delayed; often discovered on follow-up X-rays or MRI as increased bone density (sclerosis) and collapse. Patient may have had initial fracture treated conservatively.  
  *What to do:* MRI is best for early detection. If AVN is present, options include: vascularised bone grafts (e.g., 1,2 intercompartmental supraretinacular artery graft) or salvage procedures (proximal row carpectomy, scaphoid excision, four-corner fusion).
- **Post-traumatic osteoarthritis** (common if nonunion or AVN persists)  
  *Presentation:* Chronic wrist pain, decreased range of motion, radiographic joint space narrowing, osteophytes. Typically develops 5–10 years after injury.  
  *What to do:* NSAIDs, activity modification, wrist arthroscopy for debridement; eventual fusion for severe cases.
- **Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)**  
  *Presentation:* Burning pain, swelling, vasomotor changes (warmth/coolness), sudomotor changes (sweating), trophic changes. More common after surgery.  
  *What to do:* early recognition, physiotherapy, vitamin C 500 mg daily for 50 days post-injury (prophylaxis), gabapentin/pregabalin for established CRPS, consider stellate ganglion block.

**Late-presentation patterns:** A 45-year-old woman presents with "wrist pain for 6 months after a minor fall she dismissed." X-ray shows a nonunited scaphoid waist fracture with cystic changes and early AVN of the proximal pole. The stem tests "what would have been the correct initial management?" Answer: immobilisation and MRI.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **SNAC** — *Scaphoid Nonunion Advanced Collapse* pattern of wrist arthritis (stage 1: styloid involvement; stage 2: radioscaphoid; stage 3: capitate involvement; stage 4: pan-carpal).
- **A, B, C, D, E** of scaphoid fracture workup:  
  A — Anatomical snuffbox tenderness  
  B — Bone scan / MRI for occult  
  C — Casting (thumb spica)  
  D — Displacement >1 mm = surgery  
  E — Early referral to orthopaedics  

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **Most sensitive physical exam sign:** Tenderness at the anatomical snuffbox > scaphoid tubercle tenderness > axial compression of thumb. But a combination of two or more signs improves accuracy.
2. **Never accept a "normal X-ray" as ruling out scaphoid fracture** if mechanism and exam fit. The "scaphoid fat pad stripe" on a lateral X-ray (a thin lucency volar to the scaphoid) is a soft-tissue sign of haemorrhage; if present, treat as fracture.
3. **MRI is the gold standard** for occult fractures — it shows bone oedema within hours. CT is better for evaluating nonunion or displacement but not for acute diagnosis.
4. **Proximal pole fractures are emergencies** — they have the highest AVN risk and usually need prompt surgical fixation even if nondisplaced.
5. **A delay in diagnosis >4 weeks significantly increases nonunion risk** — up to 88% nonunion vs. 10% if treated early.
6. **Thumb spica is not always enough** — waist fractures may require a long-arm thumb spica for the first 6 weeks to immobilise elbow supination/pronation (which strains the scaphoid). Recent evidence is shifting but still debated.
7. **Vitamin C prophylaxis for CRPS** — give 500 mg daily for 50 days to any patient with a wrist fracture (scaphoid or distal radius) to reduce CRPS incidence.

**Common mistakes:**
- Forgetting to order a specific scaphoid view (ulnar deviation AP) on X-ray.
- Assuming a scaphoid fracture is "just a wrist sprain" because they're young.
- Not arranging a follow-up MRI for a clinically suspicious but X-ray-negative wrist.
- Mobilising a patient with a cast and no follow-up plan — they need clear return-to-clinic instructions.
- Missing associated injuries (e.g., trans-scaphoid perilunate dislocation) — look for lunate dislocation on lateral X-ray.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (orthopaedic registrar) immediately for:**
  - Open fracture (rare but possible)
  - Neurovascular compromise (median/radial nerve symptoms, absent radial pulse)
  - Suspected compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive extension of fingers)
  - Displaced fracture (step >1 mm or angulation) on X-ray — needs urgent reduction and fixation
  - Proximal pole fracture — discuss within 24 hours for possible acute fixation
- **Refer to hand surgery clinic for:**
  - All confirmed scaphoid fractures (within 1–2 days for cast vs. surgery decision)
  - Persistent nonunion or AVN findings
- **Escalate to ICU/critical care if:** 
  - Septic shock from an open fracture (very rare)
  - Massive haemorrhage from associated radial artery injury (extremely rare)
- **Thresholds to act on:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** — escalate to ward registrar; if ≥ 7, consider critical care
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in trauma context — may indicate arterial injury or compartment syndrome
  - **GCS drop** — never a direct complication of scaphoid fracture, but if associated with head injury from the same fall, escalate accordingly
